topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored envelope felt alien in my hand, heavy with the weight of forgotten time. It arrived on a Tuesday, completely unannounced, bearing a familiar, elegant script that sent a cold tremor through my chest. After fifteen years of silence, after countless times I’d rehearsed the sharp retort I’d never actually uttered, I finally broke the seal. The paper inside was thin, almost fragile, and the words began: "Please accept this belated expression of regret regarding the events of that autumn." My fingers tightened around the edges until the paper crinkled. A slow, burning heat rose up my neck. I reread the carefully chosen sentences, the measured tone of the apology, and felt a sudden, sharp need to crumple the letter into a tight ball and throw it against the wall. The silence of my apartment amplified the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I was sorting through old tax documents when the mail slot rattled, a sound so jarring it made me jump. It was an address I hadn't seen in a decade. Curiosity, a dangerous thing, pulled me toward the box. The letter was formal, almost painfully so, detailing a sincere acknowledgment of past failings. As I read the prose, a tight knot formed in my stomach, constricting my diaphragm. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my living room, my boots thudding against the hardwood floor. I didn't want to look at the handwriting, didn't want to process the sincerity; all I wanted was to find a way to destroy the evidence of this unwelcome intrusion.

[story 3]

The afternoon light slanted across my desk, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air, when the postman delivered the package. It was a simple card, folded precisely, bearing a handwritten apology addressed to me. My breath hitched. For years, I had built walls around that memory, meticulously smoothing over the jagged edges of what had transpired. Now, this unsolicited message, years too late, managed to find its way through. I picked it up, my hand trembling slightly. I turned the card over, studying the apology as if it were a complex, flawed equation. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a sound that held far more frustration than any actual word could manage. I dropped the card onto the floor, the sound echoing hollowly in the quiet room, and walked toward the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape.
